title: Generative Posterior Networks for Approximately Bayesian Epistemic Uncertainty Estimation

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - GENERATIVE POSTERIOR NETWORKS FOR APPROXI - MATELY BAYESIAN EPISTEMIC UNCERTAINTY ESTI MATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Ensembles of neural networks are often used to estimate epistemic uncertainty in high-dimensional problems because of their scalability and ease of use. These methods, however, are expensive to sample from as each sample requires a new neural network to be trained from scratch. We propose a new method, Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a generative model that, given a prior distribution over functions, approximates the posterior distribution directly by regularizing the network towards samples from the prior. This allows our method to quickly sample from the posterior and construct confidence bounds. We prove theoretically that our method indeed approximates the Bayesian posterior and show empirically that it improves epistemic uncertainty estimation over competing methods.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep learning systems are becoming ubiquitous in many industries, specifically in applications with large datasets and where prediction mistakes are inexpensive. However, for many other industries, such as autonomous manufacturing, where datasets are smaller and mistakes can be catastrophic, the adoption of deep learning techniques has been slow. One of the critical challenges to applying deep learning models in these safety-critical domains is the lack of uncertainty estimation. If a model makes a prediction in a safety critical environment, we would like that model to be highly confident that prediction is correct. Uncertainty prediction can also provide significant benefit in data-poor domains by guiding exploration towards areas of uncertainty. Most deep learning models, however, are not able to estimate epistemic uncertainty – uncertainty deriving from lack of data samples. In this work, we introduce a new method for modeling epistemic uncertainty in neural networks.

We formulate this problem as a Bayesian Inference problem: given a prior distribution over functions and a set of training data, we want to model the posterior function distribution. There are many existing approaches to constructing such a posterior, such as Gaussian Processes (GPs) or Variational Inference (VI) methods, but these methods can require significant engineering to work in practice and can be expensive in high-dimensional settings. For this reason, many practitioners turn to using

ensembles of neural networks to approximate epistemic uncertainty for their ease of use in highdimensional settings. Pearce et al. (2020) illustrated that, given the correct regularization, these ensemble members approximate samples from the posterior distribution of functions. However, using this technique, every new sample of the posterior requires training a new neural network from scratch, which can be prohibitively expensive for large problems.

To address this challenge, we introduce Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a generative neural network model that directly approximates the posterior distribution by regularizing the output of the network towards samples from the prior distribution. By learning a low-dimensional latent representation of the posterior, our method can quickly sample from the posterior and construct confidence intervals. We prove that our method approximates the Bayesian posterior over functions and show empirically that our method can improves uncertainty estimation over competing methods.

## 2 RELATED WORK

There are two categories of uncertainty in the context of probabilistic modeling: aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty. Aleatoric uncertainty refers to uncertainty inherent in a system, even when the true parameters of the system are known, while epistemic uncertainty refers to modeling uncertainty that can be reduced through collecting more data (Der Kiureghian & Ditlevsen, 2009; Gal, 2016). Deep learning models have shown impressive performance in quantifying aleatoric uncertainty when given enough data. On the other hand, despite many methods seeking to estimate epistemic uncertainty due to its well-motivated applications in Bayesian Optimization (Snoek et al., 2012; Springenberg et al., 2016), Reinforcement Learning (Curi* et al., 2020), and adversarial robustness (Stutz et al., 2020), quantifying it remains a challenging problem for deep learning models.

Gaussian Processes (GPs) remain one of the most effective methods for epistemic uncertainty estimation in low-dimensional, low-data settings. While scalability can be improved with interpolation methods (Hensman et al., 2013; Titsias, 2009; Wilson & Nickisch, 2015) and kernel learning methods (Wilson et al., 2016), GPs tend to perform worse than neural network-based methods on high-dimensional problems.

Another way to formulate epistemic uncertainty estimation is as a Bayesian Inference problem. Specifically, given a prior distribution over functions and some labeled data, the goal is to construct a posterior distribution in the Bayesian sense. Because this posterior distribution is usually intractable to compute exactly, approximate sampling methods are used instead. Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) (Tanner & Wong, 1987) and Variational Inference (VI) (Blei et al., 2017) are some of the most common methods for posterior sampling. These methods, however, tend to struggle in high dimensional problems. The computational complexity of MCMC scales poorly with the dimension of the data and VI methods tend to require restrictive function families with strong independence assumptions. Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) (Blundell et al., 2015) are another way to perform Bayesian Inference a neural networks by modeling each parameter in the network with an independent Gaussian distribution. While BNNs perform well in low dimensional settings, their performance tends to degrade in high-dimensional settings that require large networks.

Gal & Ghahramani (2016) proved that dropout can also be used as a form of Bayesian Inference. Specifically, if a network is trained using dropout and L2 regularization, posterior samples can be collected by applying dropout at test-time. This method has surprisingly good out-of-distribution detection and can be applied to any network. However, it can be hard to tune the dropout parameter to have good uncertainty prediction without significantly degrading performance.

Neural network ensembling is another commonly used epistemic uncertainty estimation technique, which is closely related to our proposed method in this work. This technique involves training a number of completely separate neural networks on the same data to form an ensemble. Because of the stochasticity of initialization and the training process, each member of the ensemble should be a slightly different function. Specifically, the ensemble members should produce similar outputs in data-dense regions of the state-space and dissimilar outputs in data-sparse regions. Pearce et al. (2020) proved that, by using the correct regularization, each ensemble member becomes an approximate sample from the function posterior. Ensembling methods are particularly attractive to practitioners because they require very little fine-tuning and work surprisingly well in practice. However, these methods have one main drawback, namely that each new sample from the posterior

## 3.1 RANDOMIZED MAP SAMPLING (RMS)

Since the values _**µ**_ like and **Σ** like are intractible to compute in practice, the challenge of computing the posterior distribution remains. However, Pearce et al. (2020) showed how we can use a method called Randomized MAP sampling (RMS) to approximately sample from the posterior distribution. With RMS we first assume access to a mechanism to compute the maximum a posteriori (MAP) solution for our posterior. Then, by randomly shifting the prior distribution we use in our MAP solver, we can estimate samples from the posterior.

Specifically, RMS samples “anchor points” _**θ**_ anc from some distribution _**θ**_ anc _∼N_ ( _**µ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** anc). Each anchor point defines a shifted prior distribution _P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc) = _N_ ( _**θ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** prior). Now we define the MAP function which finds the MAP solution using this shifted prior:

By sampling different anchor points, we can then construct a probability distribution over MAP solutions _P_ ( _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc)), which can be shown to be Gaussian. Using moment matching, Pearce et al. (2020) proved, in Theorem 1, that if we choose _**µ**_ anc = _**µ**_ prior and **Σ** anc = **Σ** prior + **Σ** prior **Σ** _[−]_ like[1] **[Σ]**[prior][,] then _P_ ( _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc)) = _P_ post( _**θ** |_ **y** obs). Using the approximation **Σ** anc _≈_ **Σ** prior, a sample from posterior, _**θ**_ post, can be collected by sampling _**θ**_ anc _∼N_ ( _**µ**_ anc _,_ **Σ** anc) and optimizing the following:

## 4 GENERATIVE POSTERIOR NETWORKS

While RMS can be used to construct samples from the posterior, every new sample requires solving a new optimization problem. Instead, we propose learning the MAP function itself.

Let _g_ be a neural network parameterized by some vector, _φ_ , that takes as input a sample from the anchor distribution, _**θ**_ anc _∼ P_ anc( _**θ**_ anc), and outputs _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc). If we assume that _g_ has enough expressive power to represent the MAP function, then _g_ ( _**θ**_ anc; _φ_ ) = _θ_ MAP( _**θ**_ anc) if:

In practice, however, this is a very hard optimization problem for two main reasons. (1) Optimizing in parameter space tends to be challenging for gradient-descent optimization. (2) The space of all parameters _**θ**_ anc is not only very large, but there are also many parameters _**θ**_ anc that map to the same outputs for all inputs. Moreover, we would expect the posterior parameters to be highly correlated and, therefore, need less expressive power to be represented.

To address these two problems, we will first change the problem definition slightly to find the posterior in _output_ space as opposed to _parameter_ space. We prove in the next section that, by changing our loss function, we can still use RMS to approximate the posterior in output space. Next, we show practically how we construct a low-dimensional representation of the anchor distribution.

## 4.3 LOW-DIMENSIONAL EMBEDDING

As mentioned above, optimizing the generative function _g_ over the the space of all anchor parameters _**θ**_ anc did not yield great results because the space was too large. Instead, we sought to learn a lowdimensional representation of the anchor parameters. There are two key reasons why we would expect to be able to decrease the representational power of the anchor distribution and maintain the same of fidelity in the posterior estimation. (1) Because of the nature of neural networks, there are many settings of parameters _**θ**_ that would result in the same output vector **Y[ˆ]** . Because we are focusing on the posterior of **Y[ˆ]** , we need less representational power to model **Y[ˆ]** . (2) And, maybe more importantly, because the posterior parameters are highly correlated, we would expect to need less expressive power to represent the posterior distribution than the prior distribution.

Thus, we would like to construct a simpler estimate of the prior space using a low-dimensional embedding vector, **z** . That is, we want our generative model to take as input the embedding vector **z** _∼N_ (0 _, I_ ), instead of _**θ**_ anc, in order to estimate the MAP function. To do this, we need to learn a mapping from anchor parameters _**θ**_ anc to embedding vectors **z** . For our experiments, we used a 1-1 embedding scheme where we sample _k_ parameters from the true prior _**θ**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**θ** k ∼ P_ prior( _**θ**_ ) and _k_ independent samples from our embedding **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ . We then jointly optimize over _**φ**_ and **z** 1 _, . . . ,_ **z** _k_ as follows:

## 4.4 CLASSIFICATION

Our method uses the assumption that the prior over **Y[ˆ]** is approximately normally distributed. While this approximation is exact, assuming the prior parameters are normally distributed, only when either the network is linear or the final layer is infinitely wide and does not have an activation function, for most deep networks, this is a good approximation. However, if we add a soft-max to the final output, as is usually done for classification tasks, this assumption is violated. To get around this, for classification tasks, we add the anchor loss to the pre-softmax outputs.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

The goal of our experiments is to illustrate the ability of our method to accurately model epistemic uncertainty for out of distribution data while retaining high performance on in distribution data. We will test our method on three problems: (1) a small-scale regression task, (2) Bayesian Optimization (BO), and (3) Classification with out of distribution examples.

We compare our method against 3 competing Bayesian methods: Gaussian Processes (GPs), Bayesian Dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016), and anchor-regularized neural-network ensembles (Pearce et al., 2020) with 10 ensemble members. For our BO experiments we use exact GP with a static kernel since the problems are sufficiently low-dimensional, but for our classification experiments we need to use approximate GPs. Specifically, we use a grid-interpolated GP with Deep Kernel Learning (Wilson et al., 2016) implemented with the GPyTorch library (Gardner et al., 2018). For the anchor-regularized ensembles, since every member requires a unique set of parameters (and anchor points) the number of ensemble members was limited by the memory capacity of our GPUs.

## 5.1 SMALL SCALE REGRESSION

Our first experiment is a small 1-dimensional regression problem where we can easily compute the ground truth. We provide each method with the same 6 observations. For this experiment, we compute 100 samples from the approximate posterior for each method (except for the Ensemble where we only use 10 samples). For ground truth, we use the Metropolis-Hastings MCMC algorithm. Figure 2 shows the results.

## 5.2 BAYESIAN OPTIMIZATION

The goal of Bayesian Optimization is to find the maximum of a function in as few samples as possible. This is commonly done by first constructing a posterior over possible functions given the samples we have collected so far, then, through various methods, choosing sample points that might maximize the true function. By using different methods for predicting the posterior in BO, we can evaluate how well these methods capture epistemic uncertainty.

For all methods we use Thompson Sampling and implemented the experiments using the BOTorch library by Balandat et al. (2020). We tested each method on five standard benchmark BO functions: the Branin, Hartmann-6D, Shekel, Michalewicz-10D, and Powell functions. We normalize the output of each function between a minimum value (found by random sampling) and the optimal value. To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that 100 prior samples from each method have a range of roughly [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1]. To choose the best regularization hyper-parameter, we ran each method with 5 different regularization parameters, then chose the hyper-parameters with the highest average performance across all BO problems.

Figure 3 shows the best normalized function value sampled so far during optimization averaged across the 5 BO problems each ran for 5 trials. Our method significantly out-performs Random sampling and even slightly outperforms GPs, one of the best BO techniques for low-dimensional problems. Even though our method trails slightly behind Bayesian Dropout, these results still illustrate the uncertainty estimation power of our method, even on low-dimensional problems.

## 5.3 CLASSIFICATION

We also look at the problem of image classification. Along with our method having high accuracy, we are also interested in how well our method (and the baseline methods) is able to capture epistemic uncertainty. To measure this we split the dataset into two sets based on class labels: the in distribution dataset and the out of distribution dataset. During training, we only provide the labels for

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the in-distribution dataset. During testing, along with recording the accuracy on the in distribution dataset, we also sample our models on the out of distribution dataset. For such out of distribution examples, we expect the models to show high epistemic uncertainty and, thus, the samples for these out of distribution examples should have high variance.

Note that we still provide the full set of unlabeled training data to our agent. This obviously favors our method over the baseline methods as our method is the only method that is able to take advantage of this unlabeled data. However, from a practical perspective this is a reasonable setup as many realworld datasets have far more unlabeled data than labeled data.

One desired property of an epistemic uncertainty estimator is in constructing confidence sets. For the classification setting, the confidence interval (CI) we consider is the set of possible class predictions. We would expect that this confidence interval contains the true class with high probability. We also expect that, on in-distribution examples, the confidence interval _only_ contains the true label with high probability. To construct these confidence intervals, we take 100 samples from each model on every data point in the test data. We define the metrics CI-Any as the proportion of data points for which _at least one_ sample contains the correct label and CI-All as the proportion of data points for which _every_ sample predicts the correct label.

Another desired property of an epistemic uncertainty estimator is in predicting when a data point is outside the training distribution and, thus, any classification prediction made on such a data point will likely be incorrect. For out of distribution data points, we expect high sample variance from the posterior model and low sample variance from in distribution data points. Thus, variance can be used as a metric to predict which test data points are outside the training distribution. To quantify each method’s ability to perform out of distribution detection using sample variance, we construct ROC curves for each method and measure the area under the curve (AUC).

We tested all methods on both the MNIST dataset and the CIFAR-10 dataset. For MNIST, the in distribtuion classes were [0 _,_ 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 4 _,_ 5] and the out of distribution classes were [6 _,_ 7 _,_ 8 _,_ 9]. For CIFAR-10, the in distribtuion classes were [“airplane”, “automobile”, “bird”, “deer”, “dog”, “ship”] and the out-of-distribution classes were [“cat”, “frog”, “horse”, “truck”]. To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that prior distributions had near 100% CI-All. To choose the best regularization hyper-parameter, as in the BO experiments, we ran each method with 5 different regularization parameters, then chose the model with both a high validation accuracy and CI-All.

For all other methods, we take 100 samples for measuring CI-Any, CI-All, and sample variance.

Table 1 summarizes the performance of each model using the metrics of test accuracy, CI-Any, CI-All, and the ROC AUC for out of distribution detection. Full ROC curves for each method are plotted in Figure 4. While all methods are able to achieve high prediction accuracy and high _CI-All_ on _in distribution_ examples, our method is the only one to achieve high _CI-Any_ on _out of distribution_ examples, illustrating our method’s ability to construct valid confidence intervals. Additionally, our method significantly out performs all other methods on out of distribution detection.

Figure 5 shows two informative test images, one from the in distribution dataset and one from the out of distribution dataset, for both MNIST and CIFAR-10, along with box plots of sampled PMFs from each of the learned models. While all methods predict the correct label with high precision on the in distribution example, ours has a uniquely wide prediction distribution on the out of distribution example, illustrating high predicted epistemic uncertainty.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this paper we introduce Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), a method to learn a generative model of the Bayesian posterior distribution by regularizing the outputs of the network towards samples of the prior. We prove that under mild assumptions, GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior. We then show empirically that our method is not only competitive with the best uncertainty predictions techniques on small Bayesian Optimization problems, it significantly outperforms these competing methods on high-dimensional classification tasks.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs), which use a generative model to approximate the Bayesian posterior by regularizing the network towards prior-based samples. The method aims to reduce the computational cost of sampling from the posterior compared to deep ensembles.

The paper addresses a practical bottleneck in Bayesian deep learning by replacing expensive ensemble retraining with a generative model. While the empirical results on classification and BO tasks are promising, the theoretical justification for the posterior approximation relies on strong assumptions that require more rigorous scrutiny.

Overview issues:

- Unjustified Gaussianity Assumption in Output Space: In Section 4.1, the authors assume the output prior P(Y_hat) follows a multivariate normal distribution to simplify the derivation of the posterior. This assumption is justified by claiming it becomes exact for linear networks or infinitely wide networks, but deep neural networks used in experiments are neither. The paper does not analyze how the non-Gaussianity of the true output distribution affects the quality of the MAP approximation or the validity of the resulting confidence intervals. To address this, the authors should provide a sensitivity analysis or empirical evidence showing that the approximation error remains bounded for standard architectures.

- Theoretical Gap in Theorem 1: Theorem 1 claims that the limit of the MAP solutions converges to the posterior distribution, but the proof relies on the assumption that the parameter-likelihood is Gaussian. As noted in Section 3, this is not true in general for non-linear functions. The transition from the parameter-space likelihood to the output-space likelihood through Lemma 1 is also problematic because the mapping from parameters to outputs is highly non-injective. The authors must clarify the conditions under which this mapping preserves the posterior structure or provide a more general proof that does not require the parameter-likelihood to be Gaussian.

- Inconsistent Handling of Unlabeled Data: Section 5.3 mentions that the GPN method is provided with the full set of unlabeled training data, whereas baseline methods are not. This creates an unfair comparison, as the ability of GPNs to perform OOD detection may stem from this extra information rather than the proposed generative architecture. To demonstrate the true value of the GPN approach, the authors should re-run the experiments where baseline methods are also given access to the unlabeled data (e.g., through semi-supervised training or consistency regularization) to ensure the gains are attributable to the posterior approximation.

- Lack of Empirical Comparison to Variational Inference: The literature context highlights Variational Inference (VI) as a standard approach for Bayesian neural networks, yet the experimental section compares GPNs only to GPs, Dropout, and Ensembles. Given that GPNs aim to approximate the posterior directly, a comparison against a standard VI baseline (like Bayes by Backprop) is necessary to evaluate the method's efficiency and accuracy trade-offs. Without this, it is difficult to determine if GPNs offer a genuine improvement over existing approximate inference techniques or if they are merely an alternative with similar limitations.

- Demonstration of GPN scaling to high-dimensional parameter spaces: The paper argues that GPNs address the computational cost of ensembling, yet the experimental validation relies on relatively small-scale classification tasks. To substantiate the claim that GPNs are suitable for high-dimensional problems, the authors should include a scalability experiment on a standard high-dimensional benchmark, such as a ResNet-50 on ImageNet or a similar architecture. This would allow for a direct comparison of the computational overhead—training time and memory usage—between GPNs and a standard ensemble of 10-50 networks. Without this, the claim regarding efficiency in high-dimensional settings remains speculative.

- Ablation study on the low-dimensional embedding dimension: The proposed method relies on learning a low-dimensional embedding z to represent the posterior, but the paper does not characterize the sensitivity of the posterior approximation to the dimensionality of this latent space. A natural question is whether the quality of the epistemic uncertainty estimation degrades as the embedding dimension varies, or if there is a threshold below which the model fails to capture the posterior's complexity. The authors should provide a plot showing the trade-off between the embedding dimension and the ECE or OOD detection performance on the MNIST or CIFAR-10 tasks. This would provide necessary guidance for practitioners on how to configure the model for different problem complexities.

- Verification of the posterior approximation quality on a synthetic model: The theoretical derivation assumes that GPNs approximate the Bayesian posterior, yet the empirical section only evaluates this indirectly through downstream tasks like BO and OOD detection. To provide a more direct demonstration, the authors should compute the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the GPN-generated posterior and the ground-truth posterior for a fully-specified, low-dimensional Bayesian Neural Network where exact inference (e.g., via HMC) is feasible. This would explicitly show that the GPN approach is converging to the correct distribution, rather than simply learning a heuristic that happens to perform well on specific metrics.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Perform a controlled experiment where baseline methods (Ensembles, Dropout) are given access to the same unlabeled data as the GPN to isolate the source of performance gains.

- Include a comparison against a standard Variational Inference baseline to place the method in the broader context of approximate Bayesian inference.

- Provide a more rigorous discussion or empirical validation regarding the impact of the Gaussianity assumption on the posterior approximation in non-linear, finite-width networks.

- Clarify the proof of Theorem 1 to explicitly address the non-injectivity of the parameter-to-output mapping.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of Ensemble 10 performance
comments.explanation: The table reports a CI-All value of 0.0% for the Ensemble 10 method on the MNIST In-Distribution dataset. Given that the accuracy for this method is 99.0%, it is mathematically impossible for the proportion of data points where every sample predicts the correct label to be 0.0%, unless the ensemble consists of models that are all wrong, which contradicts the accuracy. Please verify the CI-All calculation for this baseline.

comments.title: Unfair comparison due to asymmetric access to unlabeled data
comments.quote: Note that we still provide the full set of unlabeled training data to our agent. This obviously favors our method over the baseline methods as our method is the only method that is able to take advantage of this unlabeled data.
comments.explanation: You explicitly acknowledge that your method has access to the full unlabeled dataset while the baselines do not. This creates a fundamental confounding variable: it is impossible to determine whether the performance gains are due to the generative architecture or simply the additional information provided. To make the results meaningful, the baselines must be evaluated under the same information constraints, for instance by incorporating the unlabeled data via semi-supervised learning.

comments.title: Inconsistent sample size comparison for uncertainty estimation
comments.quote: For this experiment, we compute 100 samples from the approximate posterior for each method (except for the Ensemble where we only use 10 samples).
comments.explanation: The decision to use 100 samples for your method while limiting the ensemble baseline to 10 samples introduces a significant bias. Epistemic uncertainty estimates from ensembles are sensitive to the number of members, and a 10-fold difference in sample size likely underestimates the ensemble's performance. Please evaluate the ensemble baseline with an equivalent number of samples (100) to ensure parity.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of theoretical convergence for GPNs
comments.quote: We prove that under mild assumptions, GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that the paper proves GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior. However, the formal results section only establishes the validity of Randomized MAP sampling (RMS) via the cited Theorem 1 from Pearce et al. (2020). Since the paper proposes GPNs as a way to learn the MAP function, but does not provide a formal proof that the GPN approximation itself converges to the true posterior, please qualify this claim to specify that the theoretical guarantee applies to the underlying RMS framework.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs) to approximate Bayesian posteriors for epistemic uncertainty estimation. It reformulates Randomized MAP sampling into a generative model trained on output-space priors. The approach offers fast posterior sampling compared to ensembles. However, the experimental setup contains a significant fairness flaw regarding unlabeled data, and the theoretical guarantees rely on asymptotic discretization and Gaussian assumptions that are not rigorously bounded in practice.

The idea of learning a generative mapping for posterior samples is interesting and addresses a real scalability bottleneck. The derivation connecting output-space regularization to posterior approximation is mathematically sound under its stated assumptions. The empirical results on Bayesian Optimization are promising. The main weakness lies in the experimental design and the gap between asymptotic theory and finite-dimensional implementation.

Overview issues:

- Unfair experimental comparison due to unlabeled data usage: Section 5.3 explicitly states that GPN uses the full set of unlabeled training data, which "obviously favors our method over the baseline methods." The baselines are not adapted to leverage this unlabeled data. This setup invalidates the claim that GPN "significantly outperforms" competing methods on high-dimensional tasks. The improvement likely stems from the semi-supervised signal rather than the posterior approximation mechanism. The authors must either adapt the baselines to use unlabeled data or reframe the experiments to compare strictly under supervised settings. If the semi-supervised advantage is the primary contribution, the paper should explicitly position GPN as a semi-supervised uncertainty estimator.

- Discretization error and finite-sample regularization: Theorem 1 relies on the limit $M \to \infty$ to equate the output-space likelihood with the parameter-space likelihood. In practice, the regularization term in Eq 15 uses a finite set of sample points. The authors assume off-diagonal covariance terms are negligible. The paper provides no analysis or bounds on how the discretization density affects posterior calibration. Readers will question how coarse the grid can be before the approximation breaks down, especially in high-dimensional spaces where uniform grids become infeasible. The authors should add a sensitivity analysis varying the number of regularization points and provide theoretical or empirical bounds on the approximation error as a function of grid density.

- Unjustified Gaussian approximation for deep networks: The theoretical derivation assumes the prior and likelihood over the output vector are Gaussian. This assumption holds exactly for linear networks or infinitely wide final layers. Standard deep networks with nonlinear activations violate it. Section 4.4 attempts to bypass this for classification by applying the loss to pre-softmax outputs. It does not address how the Gaussian assumption impacts uncertainty calibration for highly nonlinear function classes. The paper should include a calibration analysis to demonstrate that the approximation does not lead to overconfident or miscalibrated uncertainty estimates.

- Training cost and embedding optimization stability: The abstract claims ensembles are expensive to sample from because each requires training from scratch. GPN requires training a large generative model that maps anchors to outputs. The training process involves evaluating the base network repeatedly to compute the regularization term. Section 4.3 describes a joint optimization over network parameters and discrete embedding vectors. This non-convex joint optimization is prone to mode collapse or poor coverage of the anchor space, yet the paper does not discuss how the embedding size is chosen. The authors should report training compute metrics and discuss strategies to stabilize the embedding optimization.

- Worked special case for the output-space reformulation: The theory shifts from parameter space to output space, relying on a discretization grid of size $M$. The paper never computes the GPN loss or posterior approximation for a fully specified parametric model. A concrete example, such as linear regression or a shallow network with a known conjugate posterior, would let readers verify that the output-space regularization term converges to the parameter-space objective as $M$ increases. Without this, the theoretical bridge between the two spaces remains abstract. Readers cannot calibrate their intuition about the approximation quality without seeing the equations evaluated on a simple, tractable model. Providing this worked example would also clarify how the diagonal covariance approximation in practice affects the learned posterior.

- Simulation isolating approximation error from optimization error: The empirical section compares GPN against baselines on downstream tasks, but it does not isolate how well the generative network actually approximates the true posterior. A simulation on a moderate-sized dataset where the true posterior can be computed via MCMC is necessary. The authors should vary the latent embedding dimension and the number of regularization points, then measure the Wasserstein distance between the GPN samples and the MCMC ground truth. This would demonstrate whether the method's performance stems from the posterior approximation mechanism or simply from the semi-supervised signal. Such a simulation is standard for methods claiming to recover Bayesian posteriors.

- Computational complexity of the output-space regularization term: The regularization term requires computing and inverting a covariance matrix over $M$ discretization points. The paper assumes a diagonal approximation for high-dimensional problems but provides no discussion of the computational complexity as $M$ grows. Readers need to know how many grid points are feasible for image-sized inputs or high-dimensional BO domains before the diagonal assumption becomes unavoidable. The authors should report the training time and memory usage as a function of the grid size, and clarify how the method scales to problems where $M$ exceeds available GPU memory. This information is essential for practitioners deciding whether to deploy the method on their own datasets.

- Missing calibration analysis for uncertainty estimates: The abstract and conclusion claim that GPNs enable rapid posterior sampling and construct confidence bounds. However, the experimental section reports accuracy, CI-Any/CI-All, and ROC AUC for out-of-distribution detection, but omits standard calibration metrics. Uncertainty estimation claims require showing that predicted variances match empirical frequencies, such as through reliability diagrams or Expected Calibration Error. Adding these metrics would verify whether the Gaussian approximation and diagonal covariance assumption actually yield well-calibrated uncertainty estimates in practice. Without calibration results, the paper cannot substantiate its central claim about approximating the Bayesian posterior.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-run classification experiments with a fair supervised setup where all baselines receive identical labeled data, or explicitly adapt baselines to use unlabeled data.

- Add a sensitivity analysis on the number of regularization points $M$ and provide bounds on the discretization error relative to posterior calibration.

- Include Expected Calibration Error (ECE) or reliability diagrams to verify that the Gaussian output approximation does not produce miscalibrated uncertainty.

- Report training time and memory FLOPs compared to ensembles and SWAG, and detail how the embedding size $k$ is selected to prevent mode collapse.

comments.title: Flawed Objective for Learning the MAP Function
comments.quote:
If we assume that $g$ has enough expressive power to represent the MAP function, then $g(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}};\phi)=\theta_{\text{MAP}}(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}})$ if:

$\phi=\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\phi}\mathbb{E}_{\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}}\sim P_{\text{anc}}(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}})}\log P_{\text{like}}(\mathbf{y}_{\text{obs}}\,|\,g(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}};\phi))+\log P_{\text{anc}}(g(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}};\phi)).$
comments.explanation: Maximizing the expected log joint probability over $\phi$ does not recover the pointwise MAP function $g(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}}) = \theta_{\text{MAP}}(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}})$. This objective only optimizes the average posterior density, which would drive the deterministic network $g$ to collapse to a single mode (the global MAP or prior mean) rather than learning the conditional mapping from anchors to MAP solutions. To actually learn the MAP function, the training objective must enforce a correspondence between each sampled anchor and its corresponding MAP solution. You should reformulate the justification to specify a supervised learning objective where $g$ predicts pre-computed MAP solutions for sampled anchors, or explain how the current objective avoids mode collapse.

comments.title: Contradiction Between Stated Approximation and Sampling Distribution
comments.quote: Using the approximation $\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{anc}}\approx\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}}$, a sample from posterior, $\bm{\theta}_{\text{post}}$, can be collected by sampling $\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}}\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{\mu}_{\text{anc}},\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{anc}})$ and optimizing the following:
comments.explanation: The text explicitly invokes the approximation $\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{anc}} \approx \bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}}$ to simplify the sampling procedure, yet immediately instructs sampling from the exact covariance $\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{anc}}$. This defeats the purpose of the approximation, which is to avoid computing the intractable likelihood covariance $\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{like}}$. The sampling distribution must match the stated approximation to be consistent. Change the sampling instruction to $\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}}\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{\mu}_{\text{anc}},\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}})$ to align with the stated approximation.

comments.title: Missing Gaussian Likelihood Assumption for Exact Posterior Equality
comments.quote: Using moment matching, *Pearce et al. (2020)* proved, in Theorem 1, that if we choose $\bm{\mu}_{\text{anc}}=\bm{\mu}_{\text{prior}}$ and $\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{anc}}=\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}}+\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}}\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{like}}^{-1}\bm{\Sigma}_{\text{prior}}$, then $P(\theta_{\text{MAP}}(\bm{\theta}_{\text{anc}}))=P_{\text{post}}(\bm{\theta}\,|\,\mathbf{y}_{\text{obs}})$.
comments.explanation: The claim that the distribution of MAP solutions exactly equals the Bayesian posterior relies on the likelihood being Gaussian (or a valid Laplace approximation holding around the MAP). Without this assumption, the moment-matching argument only yields an approximation, not an exact equality. The proof section currently presents the equality as unconditional, which is mathematically unsound for general neural network likelihoods. Add an explicit assumption stating that the likelihood $P_{\text{like}}(\mathbf{y}_{\text{obs}} \mid \bm{\theta})$ is Gaussian after the moment-matching claim.

comments.title: Overly Restrictive Claim on Infinite-Width Gaussianity
comments.quote: only when either the network is linear or the final layer is infinitely wide and does not have an activation function
comments.explanation: The assertion that an activation function must be absent for the output prior to be exactly Gaussian at infinite width contradicts established Neural Network Gaussian Process (NNGP) theory. When weights are initialized i.i.d. from a Gaussian distribution, the Central Limit Theorem ensures that the pre-activations at any layer converge to a Gaussian process as the layer width approaches infinity, regardless of whether the activation function is ReLU, tanh, or sigmoid. The non-linearity only affects the covariance kernel of the resulting GP, not the marginal Gaussianity of the outputs. Replace "does not have an activation function" with "regardless of the activation function applied to the layer outputs" to align with standard theory.

comments.title: Unjustified Link Between Anchor Loss and Gaussian Prior Preservation
comments.quote: However, if we add a soft-max to the final output, as is usually done for classification tasks, this assumption is violated. To get around this, for classification tasks, we add the anchor loss to the pre-softmax outputs.
comments.explanation: The logical connection between the softmax violation and the proposed anchor loss solution is missing. If the prior is defined over the pre-softmax logits, applying a softmax transformation to obtain probabilities does not alter the distribution of those logits; it merely maps them to the simplex. Therefore, the Gaussian prior assumption on the pre-softmax outputs remains intact regardless of whether softmax is used downstream. The text implies that anchor loss is required to "get around" the violation, but it does not explain the mechanism. Clarify whether the Gaussian prior assumption is abandoned for classification or how the anchor loss specifically interacts with the prior regularization to maintain valid uncertainty estimates under softmax.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Ensemble Computational Cost
comments.quote: These methods, however, are expensive to sample from as each sample requires a new neural network to be trained from scratch.
comments.explanation: The abstract mischaracterizes the computational cost of ensembles. Standard ensembles consist of $K$ pre-trained networks. Drawing a sample only requires $K$ forward passes, which is computationally cheap. The real bottleneck is training those $K$ networks independently from scratch to capture diverse posterior modes. This phrasing misleads readers about the inference cost of the baseline methods. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that the expense lies in the training phase, not the forward-pass sampling phase.

comments.title: Discrepant Posterior Sample Counts Bias Uncertainty Comparison
comments.quote: For this experiment, we compute 100 samples from the approximate posterior for each method (except for the Ensemble where we only use 10 samples).
comments.explanation: Comparing 100 posterior samples for the proposed methods against only 10 samples for the Ensemble baseline introduces a severe bias in uncertainty estimation. With only 10 samples, the empirical variance and tail behavior of the ensemble's predictive distribution will be poorly estimated, likely underestimating epistemic uncertainty compared to the 100-sample runs. This discrepancy makes it impossible to determine whether performance differences stem from the algorithm or the sampling noise. Specify 100 samples for the Ensemble as well to ensure a fair comparison of posterior approximations.

comments.title: Oracle Information Leakage via Normalization
comments.quote: We normalize the output of each function between a minimum value (found by random sampling) and the optimal value.
comments.explanation: Normalizing the objective function by its true global optimal value leaks oracle information into the Bayesian Optimization loop. Standard BO protocols normalize by the best observed value so far or a fixed range known before optimization begins. Using the true optimum artificially restricts the effective search space and inflates the reported cumulative regret or best-found value. Replace this with a standard normalization scheme, such as scaling by the best observed value or a fixed known bound, to ensure the BO evaluation is methodologically sound.

comments.title: Contradiction Between Variance Goal and CI-All Tuning Target
comments.quote: To make sure that each method had a large enough sample variance, we chose hyper-parameters such that prior distributions had near 100% CI-All.
comments.explanation: This sentence contains two logical inconsistencies. First, a 100% CI-All score means every sample predicts the correct label, which corresponds to zero sample variance across the ensemble. Tuning hyperparameters to maximize CI-All will directly minimize sample variance, contradicting the stated goal of ensuring "large enough sample variance." Second, the authors refer to "prior distributions" having a CI-All score. Priors are defined before seeing data and are not typically evaluated for classification accuracy or CI-All on a validation set; this likely refers to the trained models or posterior approximations. Clarify the actual tuning objective and correct the reference to "prior distributions" as "trained models" or "posterior samples."

comments.title: Overstated Theoretical Guarantee in Conclusion
comments.quote: We prove that under mild assumptions, GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that GPNs approximate samples from the true posterior based on the provided proof. However, the theoretical analysis relies on the asymptotic limit $M \to \infty$ to equate the output-space likelihood with the parameter-space likelihood, and assumes a Gaussian approximation for the prior and likelihood that only holds exactly for linear networks or infinitely wide final layers. Presenting this result as a direct guarantee without acknowledging the asymptotic discretization requirement or the restrictive Gaussian assumption mischaracterizes the scope of the proof. Clarify that the approximation holds in the limit of dense regularization grids and under specific architectural assumptions.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Generative Posterior Networks (GPNs) as a scalable method for approximating Bayesian posteriors in neural networks. The core idea of learning a generative model of the posterior via output-space regularization is promising. However, the manuscript contains critical theoretical and methodological flaws. The primary training objective (Equation 15) does not correspond to the theoretical objective derived from the Randomized MAP (RMS) procedure, and the theoretical proofs rely on assumptions (Gaussian outputs) that are violated by the experimental setup. Additionally, the empirical evaluation omits standard baselines in scalable Bayesian deep learning.

The paper makes a significant contribution to the field of uncertainty estimation by attempting to bridge the gap between expensive ensembles and efficient generative models. The concept of learning a generative model of the posterior is theoretically sound. However, the current draft fails to establish a valid link between the proposed loss function and the theoretical guarantees. The theoretical proofs are contingent on assumptions that do not hold for the networks used in the experiments, and the empirical analysis is incomplete due to missing baselines. The work requires substantial revision to be publishable at a top-tier venue.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical disconnect between training objective and RMS procedure: The paper claims that minimizing the loss function in Equation 15 corresponds to maximizing the expected log-posterior (Equation 13). However, the regularization term $ eta || oldsymbol{\delta}||^2$ penalizes the distance between the model output $g$ and the true function $f$ at sampled points, whereas the theoretical objective requires maximizing the anchor prior $ oldsymbol{	heta}_{anc}$. The derivation in the text (referencing Appendix A.3) incorrectly equates the anchor prior term with a consistency regularizer. This fundamental mismatch means the method is likely learning a smooth interpolant of the training data rather than the MAP function defined by the RMS procedure, undermining the core claim that GPNs approximate the Bayesian posterior.

- Theoretical assumptions do not match experimental setup: Theorem 1 states that the method approximates the posterior provided the output prior $P( oldsymbol{	ilde{Y}})$ is Gaussian. The paper explicitly notes this is only exact for linear functions or networks with infinitely wide final layers. However, the experiments utilize standard convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on MNIST and CIFAR-10, which are neither linear nor infinitely wide. The theoretical guarantee provided in the paper does not extend to the models used in the experiments. The authors must either relax the theoretical assumptions to account for finite-width networks or provide a new theoretical justification for why the method works empirically despite the Gaussian approximation.

- Missing standard baselines for scalable Bayesian deep learning: The empirical evaluation compares GPNs against Gaussian Processes, Bayesian Dropout, and standard ensembles. However, the paper omits two major competitors in the field of scalable Bayesian deep learning: Stochastic Weight Averaging Gaussian (SWAG) and Laplace approximations. SWAG and Laplace are specifically designed to approximate the posterior from SGD iterates and are standard baselines for methods claiming to improve upon ensembles or MC Dropout. Omitting these baselines makes the empirical contribution incomplete and difficult to contextualize within the current literature.

- Ad-hoc handling of classification lacks theoretical justification: The paper states that for classification tasks, the Gaussian assumption is violated and the authors 'add the anchor loss to the pre-softmax outputs.' This modification is presented as a practical fix but lacks any theoretical derivation or justification. There is no explanation of how this adjustment preserves the theoretical properties of the RMS procedure or ensures that the learned posterior is valid for categorical distributions. This section reads as a heuristic patch rather than a rigorous extension of the method.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting methodological direction, but the theoretical foundation is currently flawed and the empirical evaluation is incomplete. The authors must correct the theoretical connection between the loss function and the RMS objective and include the missing baselines before the work can be considered for publication.
